data.title: Eastman Chemical: Spending Cuts And China Détente Support Shares (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Eastman Chemical Company (NYSE:EMN) have been a significant underperformer over the past year, losing a quarter of their value and sitting near 52-week lows. The entire chemicals sector has performed poorly, amid concerns about global growth, weak Chinese construction activity, and the potential negative ramifications of US tariffs. I last covered shares of Eastman in April, rating the stock a "sell." While EMN has yet to move into the mid-$60's, the stock has been a tremendous underperformer, gaining just 2% while the market has rallied back 20%, justifying the sell rating. Given how much the stock has lagged, it is worth seeing if shares may now offer a better relative opportunity.

Over the past three months, markets have been focused intensely on what the tariff policy of the United States will ultimately be, and as of now, there is still considerable uncertainty. While a return to uniform, retaliatory tariffs is highly unlikely in my view, we are nearing the end of the 90-day pause during which there is a 10% baseline tariff. So far, only the UK has secured a deal. Over the next two weeks, there will likely be a scramble for deals, some delays, and some incremental tariff increases, depending on the country.

Eastman is a relatively unique company in this context because it is a net exporter. As such, its business is more impacted by retaliatory tariffs than by US tariffs. On the Q1 earnings call, management cited $30 million of Q2 tariff costs. It did not provide H2 guidance, given the unpredictability of where tariffs will land. Over the past two months, I believe the company has received some incremental good news on tariffs.

As a net exporter, one of its biggest exposures is on sales to China. Indeed, 60% of China's sales are supplied from the U.S. During the peak of tariff tensions, there was a 100+% tariff on these imports, which essentially made it impossible to sell product to China, greatly impairing its business. As part of the current truce, there is just a 10% tariff from China on its US Imports. This is far more manageable, and given US cost advantages due to lower natural gas, a 10% tariff is not likely to materially reduce US chemical exports to China in my view. Now, there is considerable uncertainty as to how long this truce can last, or if each nation is buying time while trying to secure other supplies for key products. However, if tariffs remain at 10%, this would be better than I feared in early April.

That said, there are still second-round tariff impacts that impact EMN. Most notably, tariffs do risk reducing economic activity, which in turn will weaken chemical companies' earnings power. EMN has meaningful cyclical exposure. Construction is about 11% of revenue, with autos close to 20%. The auto sector appears likely to face a persistent 25% tariff, while businesses may delay cap-ex plans amid high rates and economic uncertainty. As you can see below, construction spending has been steadily slowing and is now in negative territory.

While not necessarily a "tariff cost," the extent to which the industrial economy is slowing is an ongoing headwind for the business that I expect to persist. It also appears unlikely that the Fed will be meaningfully reducing interest rates, which will continue to weigh on construction sector activity and EMN results by proxy.

Even as overall the tariff posture is better than feared, especially regarding China, there has other incremental negative news. As part of its effort to roll back Biden-era energy policies, the Trump Administration is revoking a $375 million grant for an EMN facility in Texas. On the last earnings call, EMN management was confident that funding would persist. This has proven not to be the case, questioning the future viability of the project. It is scheduled to be completed in 2028. Given the multiyear nature of the project, there is no near-term revenue impact, and I would expect the company to defer work on it or even cancel the project entirely if there is no legal recourse to restore funding.

Looking at recent results, in the company's first quarter reported on April 24th, Eastman earned $1.91, which did beat estimates by $0.02 even as revenue was down 1% to $2.3 billion. EMN generated $311 million of EBIT, up $6 million sequentially, with margins holding flat at 13.6%. Volumes fell by 1%.

I would emphasize that Q1 results do not have any tariff impact, which will begin to hit results this quarter. Indeed, it is guiding to $1.70-$1.90 in Q2 EPS, which was about $0.40 below consensus at the time. EMN results usually improve sequentially in Q2, given the seasonality of construction spending as weather improves, but that appears unlikely this year. Management is no longer providing full-year EPS guidance, given the magnitude of uncertainties.

Because of the weak environment, EMN is redoubling its effort to cut costs. It is halfway through a $50 million cost-cutting program. Given economic uncertainty, it is upsizing the plan to achieve $75 million of run-rate cost savings. Additionally, EMN cut its 2025 cap-ex plan to $550 million from $700-800 million. With funding pulled for its TX facility, further cap-ex cuts are possible.

Its advanced materials unit is an example of how cost-cutting is helping EMN manage through the down cycle. As you can see, revenue was down 4% from last year, with both volumes and pricing down. However, EBIT was up $9 million thanks to 120bps of margin expansion. A significant part of this was its Kingsport facility ramping up activity. Its new Kingsport facility is performing well, providing $75 million of EBITDA uplift this year from 2024. Still, weakness in the auto market and in China clouds the go-forward outlook.

Its additives business was the relative bright spot, with revenue up 4% from last year, thanks to better volumes and pricing. Margins expanded 80bps, leading to a 10% jump in EBIT to $141 million.

Destocking has hurt its fiber business, which was down 12% on a volume basis. Revenue declined by 13% to $288 million, leading to a $15 million drop in EBIT to $88 million. Chinese demand for textiles was particularly weak. This is an area where EMN may struggle indirectly from tariffs. With a 55% tariff from the US on China imports, Chinese apparel manufacturers are likely to face substantial headwinds, which will reduce their need to buy fibers from EMN. I view the apparel sector as the most exposed to tariffs.

Importantly, for this period of uncertainty, EMN has a solid balance sheet with 2.3x debt to EBITDA. It has fairly well-stagged maturities, though refinancing its 2026 maturity next year will likely lead to some modest incremental interest expense. Given its solid balance sheet, I view its 4.4% dividend as secure, and management targets a modest $100-200 million of buybacks.

Back in April, I was targeting $6.25 in go-forward earnings power and $450 million of free cash flow. I did expect calendar 2025 results to be a bit stronger than this, due to the fact tariffs only began to hit the business in Q2. This run-rate implied about $6.75 in 2025 EPS. With some incremental cost cuts and a less aggressive retaliatory stance from China, I view the macro backdrop a bit better.

As such, I now expect about $7.00 of calendar year EPS and ~$6.50 on a 12-month forward basis. This remains a bit below the $7.40 consensus, but with shares having underperformed so much, I believe markets are already pricing in some future downward revisions. I do remain very cautious on the ~10% growth analysts assume next year, as I do not see signs of a reacceleration in industrial activity.

Importantly, with a much lower cap-ex trajectory, this business should generate at least $650 million of free cash flow now, and I see scope for EMN to continue deferring expansion activities. This leaves the stock with a ~7.5% free cash flow yield. I have generally valued commodity chemical companies with solid balance sheets in the 7-8% zone.

As a result, unless we see a recession, which causes another leg lower in revenue, it appears the stock is now more adequately pricing in the current environment. Between some moderation on trade and aggressive cost-cut actions, EMN results may not be as bad as once feared, even as I do not see material recovery coming. Having already seen such significant relatively underperformance, I do not believe EMN merits a sell rating at this point.

As such, I am moving shares to a "hold" with the stock fairly valued. I see no rush to buy shares, given the slow recovery and modest buyback activity—I would wait for a dip in the $60's to buy, should such an opportunity to materialize. But, shares have sufficiently de-risked relative to the market that selling is no longer appropriate at this time.

data.title: CDX: Good Idea, Bad Execution
data.description:
Back in November, I recommended the Simplify High Yield ETF (CDX) as a good way to own high-yield bonds, as the fund uses an equity hedge to protect against downside risks. However, given my recent disappointment with the Simplify Enhanced Income ETF (HIGH), I wanted to revisit the CDX ETF, to make sure Simplify continues to manage the fund as per its mandate.

At first glance, the CDX ETF appears to be doing okay, delivering 5.6% in total returns since November, even better than the S&P 500 Index (Figure 1).

However, there are two notable periods in the fund's price history that are worth investigating (Figure 2).

The first sharp drop in CDX's price history occurred on December 23, 2024. This one is easy to explain, as the CDX paid a large $0.9471 / share distribution that went ex. dividend on the date (Figure 3).

The second period of volatility occurred in early April, and understanding this volatility requires us to revisit CDX's strategy.

Brief Fund Overview

For those not familiar with the CDX ETF, the Simplify High Yield PLUS Credit Hedge ETF is a high-yield bond fund that owns total return swaps that give the fund exposure to high-yield bonds.

The CDX ETF has $236 million in AUM and charges a discounted net expense ratio of 0.25% until February 2026 (Figure 4).

The CDX ETF also hedges extreme credit downside by holding a basket of high-quality stocks and shorting a basket of low-quality ("junk") stocks (Figure 5).

In Simplify's opinion, this quality/junk ("Q-J") hedge is superior to traditional credit hedges like credit default swaps ("CDS") or put options on high-yield ETFs like the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) because it does not require the CDX ETF to pay a 'carry'.

Most forms of hedges, such as CDS or put options, require the hedger to pay periodic 'carry', akin to insurance premiums. Thus, hedges tend to cost money overall, and are called 'negative carry'. However, CDX's Q-J hedge requires no periodic payments and has historically delivered positive total returns in backtests, so it is considered a 'positive carry' hedge (Figure 6).

Hedges Can Get 'Yippy' At Extremes

On paper, the CDX ETF looks like a fantastic product, since investors can gain exposure to high-yield bonds with a downside hedge against extreme market events. However, borrowing a term from President Trump, markets can get 'yippy' at extremes, and what looks good on paper may not work in reality.

The volatile price action we saw in early April, down 7% from April 2nd to April 4th, up 14% from April 4th to April 9th, and down 9% on April 10th, is likely the result of 'basis risk' (Figure 7).

As I wrote in my prior article:

Furthermore, another risk to CDX is the basis between credit and equity risk. Effectively, CDX is trying to use the heightened equity downside risks on low-quality stocks to hedge the credit risk in its high-yield bond portfolio. While the theory is good, in practice, there may be periods when this hedge will not work.

This is essentially what happened in early April.

The good news is that the CDX ETF appears to have emerged relatively unscathed from Trump's 'Liberation Day' and has vastly outperformed other credit funds like the HYG ETF or the Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB). CDX has proved the naysayers wrong.

However, the bad news is that if investors experienced the plunge on April 4th, they could have panicked and sold their shares, missing the subsequent rebound. Investors considering the CDX ETF must have trust in the portfolio management team at Simplify to adhere to their mandate and 'ride through' any short-term volatility.

Trust Takes Years To Earn, But Can Be Lost Overnight

Unfortunately, as I have alluded to at the beginning of this article, I have recently lost some faith in Simplify's portfolio managers after repeatedly seeing them deviate from their assigned mandates. For example, the HIGH ETF was supposed to sell spreads on equity and credit indices, but was found to have sold directional puts on highly volatile single stocks like Strategy (MSTR), which directly led to a steep drawdown after Trump's 'Liberation Day' (Figure 8).

Similarly, a look at CDX's portfolio shows signs of mandate creep (Figure 9).

While the core positions in the Q-J hedge and HYG total return swaps are still present, we also see a curious new position, "OTC USDcHKDp 7.80 7/8/25 JPM". Although my OTC security naming skills are a little rusty, this looks like an OTC option bet on the USD/HKD exchange rate, struck with JPMorgan.

Putting aside the investment rationale behind this bet, perhaps the manager has a view that the Hong Kong Dollar will de-peg from the U.S. dollar over the next year, we should ask the basic question: Is this part of the mandate?

To my understanding, the CDX ETF's mandate is to deliver the total returns of high-yield bonds, hedged against adverse credit events with a long-short portfolio of high-quality stocks against low-quality stocks. The CDX ETF can also opportunistically use traditional credit hedges like CDS and put options. Nowhere in this understanding is the mention of speculation in foreign exchange derivatives.

Once again, we see the Simplify portfolio managers deviating from their mandates. In my opinion, if Mike Green, the PM for the CDX ETF, feels strongly about the USD/HKD bet, he should place it in an unconstrained macro fund that is designed to bet in any sector/strategy; something like his defunct Simplify Macro Strategy ETF, for example. He should not muddy the management of the CDX ETF with trades outside of its mandate.

Conclusion

Revisiting the CDX ETF after 6 months, I am happy to report that the CDX ETF has delivered strong returns, solidly outperforming other credit strategies and proving naysayers wrong.

However, similar to other Simplify ETFs like the HIGH and SVOL ETFs, we see mandate creep with the CDX ETF holding positions that do not align with its mandate.

I continue to believe CDX's strategy of using a long-quality/short-junk equity portfolio to hedge credit risk is a brilliant idea. However, due to heightened operational risk with Simplify's portfolio managers, I am downgrading the CDX ETF to a hold recommendation.

data.title: Apple Stock Price Struggles To Rebound As Price Slips Toward $194 Support
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL, TSX:AAPL:CA) shares continued to face headwinds on Wednesday, closing at $196.08 as the stock struggled to reclaim the psychologically significant $200 threshold. The price has remained locked in a broader declining structure since March, with lower highs compressing against a weakening base around the $195 zone.

Bearish technicals dominate despite brief support hold

The technical landscape shows Apple’s recent failure to sustain upward momentum. The price remains below its 20-day and 50-day exponential moving averages, both of which are trending lower. Repeated rejections near the $197.50–$198.00 range have capped rebound efforts, while the 100- and 200-day EMAs at $204 and $211 remain distant upside targets.

Bollinger Bands are narrowing sharply, and the price is hugging the lower band, a sign of low volatility and limited buying interest. Momentum indicators remain weak. The daily RSI is flat near 47, and the MACD histogram continues to show subdued momentum, while Parabolic SAR dots remain above price on lower timeframes. The directional movement index suggests a weak trend, with sellers still slightly in control.

Support at risk as selling pressure persists

The $194–$195 zone has emerged as short-term support, but repeated tests are eroding its strength. A close below $194 could trigger a deeper decline toward the $190.28 Fibonacci pivot, followed by $186 or even $180, where historical demand may reappear. On the upside, bulls would need to reclaim $198.50 and close above $200 to stabilize the trend. A further break above $204.50 would be needed to re-engage any sustained bullish momentum.

In earlier reports, we highlighted the ongoing bearish alignment in Apple’s chart and flagged the $194–$195 zone as a critical defense line. The current structure confirms that unless this area holds, risk remains tilted to the downside into late June.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: FNGB: High Costs And High Risk
data.description:
Introduction

MicroSectors FANG+ 3x Leveraged ETN (NYSEARCA:FNGB) is a triple leveraged ETN that aims to be linked to the performance of the NYSE FANG+ Index. This is an investment for experienced traders who want to maximize leverage on the performance of the underlying index, while keeping in mind the costs and the short-term principles of this fund. Per the prospectus itself, the fund is expected to be a short-term investment. In terms of the underlying index, there are 10 highly traded growth stocks that are roughly equally weighted in the ETN, and these are companies that are focused on tech, AI, and communications. Please see below for some key details, taken from the prospectus.

  • AUM: $1.6B
  • Daily Investor's Fee: 0.95% per annum, accrued on a daily basis
  • Rebalance Frequency: Quarterly

Their top 10 holdings, per Seeking Alpha, are as follows.

These holdings are spread out over the following sectors.

Again, this is a strategy that should be focused on short-term plays only. The names in the Index, that the ETN follows, are all growth names and companies that we like - but remember that because it is an ETN, they do not "own" it themselves.

Top Index Holdings Analysis

Broadcom (AVGO): AVGO is a leading AI play that focuses on data center connectivity and custom chips for its clients. This firm is consistently innovating and has shown strong earnings growth over the past few years with the AI wave. Recently, the firm launched their VMware Cloud Foundation 9.0 platform, which will help to expand its cloud infrastructure stack and help more of its clients conduct business over its servers. We view this as important because not only is AI development dependent on chips and hardware, the software and the cloud computing elements are just as critical to a well-rounded, functioning AI tech stack. Additionally, AVGO is consistently placing itself at the forefront of future technologies. For example, the firm recently has been making waves in the field of co-packaged optics, which is being driven by the massive uptick in AI investments over the past few years. These products can be used in visual analysis powered by AI in the future, which has implications in smart cities, autonomous vehicles, and a wide array of military use cases as well. AVGO is viewed by many as a fantastic way to gain exposure to AI computing and their products are competing against both Nvidia and AMD - and it is good to see their inclusion in the index that FNGB tracks and follows.

CrowdStrike (CRWD): Last summer, the company was mired in lawsuits over its world-famous outage that derailed many industries, including travel. Just this week, the company revealed that it had defeated the Air Traveler class action lawsuit over its outages, which is a big boon for the company - at least in PR value. This stagnant cloud finally being lifted is great news for the company and at minimum, clears them from any 'wrongdoing' per se in regard to the outages. In terms of its products today, the company is leveraging agentic AI to power cloud detection and hasten responses to hacks and breach situations. The firm states that cloud intrusions were up 26% in 2024, and they are confident that by using AI to find patterns and detect possible breakthroughs, they will be able to find, triage, and address the problem faster than ever before. Cybersecurity is an absolutely critical component of the increasingly digital and AI-driven world than ever before, and it remains paramount that companies like CRWD are operating at peak efficiency and able to counterattack before anything gets out of hand.

This chart, showing the average number of weekly cyberattacks per organization, marks a clear uptrend in the amount of online attacks that occur on a regular basis. This clearly outlines the trend that virtual threats are more and more important to consider for organizations, and companies like CRWD help to prevent these from becoming even more intrusive and damaging than they already are. We are happy, therefore, to see the index hold CRWD.

Netflix (NFLX): NFLX remains a top name in entertainment. The firm recently partnered with the French network TF1 - showing how dominant the company is in terms of global impact. This could be a case where NFLX uses this deal as a launchpad to corner more pieces of the overall market in various countries, by signing deals with the leading networks in each region. This continued diversification into live TV can help to broaden the firm's revenues from streaming and development. This type of deal is a great precedent to set for NFLX around the world, and we think that there may be more partnerships like this to follow soon. All of these strategies help NFLX to continually gain users, as shown in the picture below.

This trend of upward growth, despite the competition that stems from the many streaming sites out there currently, shows that the innovation at NFLX is not stopping and will continue in the future. Their internal development of original content is top-notch as well, and it has been powering their capture of market share over the past few years - see the graph below.

Overall, we believe that NFLX will continue to grow and dominate the market - and therefore, we are pleased to see that the index has NFLX as a top holding.

Risks

Leverage

As a leveraged ETN, FNGB will have unique returns and risks that investors would not see with a non-leveraged investment. If a non-leveraged investment loses value on one day, it would need to have a larger increase (on a percentage basis) on the next day in order for investors to break even. For example, if my investment lost 10% today, it would need to increase by 11.11% tomorrow for me to break even. With a leveraged investment like FNGB, this relationship also holds true. However, if you look at it from the perspective of the underlying security (in this case, the FANG+ index), you see a different picture.

If the FANG+ index lost 10% today, then FNGB investors would lose 30%. In order to breakeven, FNGB would need to increase in value by 42.85%, meaning that the FANG+ index would need to increase by 42.85 / 3 = 14.29%.

FANG+ Movement

FANG+ Investor Loss

FNGB Investor Loss

FANG+ Investor breakeven gain

FNGB Investor breakeven gain

-10%

-10%

-30%

11.11%

14.29%

As you can see, it is much harder to recoup a loss when you own a leveraged investment than it would be for a non-leveraged investment.

Volatility

Due to the leveraged nature of the ETN, volatility can also play a factor in returns. As we discussed, the losses from a leveraged security are harder to recoup, so if it has higher volatility, you might see more negative days and have compounding losses. In the FNGB fact sheet, MicroSectors even states, "The ETNs seek a return on the underlying index for a single day. The ETNs are not "buy and hold" investments and should not be expected to provide its respective return of the underlying index's cumulative return for periods greater than a day."

ETNs are debt

ETNs are technically a type of bond that has no interest payments, but will pay out the returns from the tracked index (minus any fees) upon maturity. Unlike ETFs, the issuers of an ETN are not required to actually buy or hold the security it aims to track. This means that investors are fully dependent on the ETN issuer to have the funds available to pay out returns.

This means that the value of the ETN could also deviate from the underlying security it is tracking. For example, if the issuer has a bad debt rating or liquidity issues, the risk of repayment is higher and the ETN might lose value even if the underlying index has not.

Conclusion

We like the underlying FANG+ index. It holds some of the best names in the tech, AI, communications, and entertainment sectors. These companies are leaders in their field, have shown steady growth over the years, and are continuing to innovate and diversify.

However, we assert that an ETF that tracks the same or similar index would be a better way to get exposure to the FANG+ companies. Considering that the index only has 10 stocks, it could even be better to just buy those 10 stocks outright and avoid any management fees. While ETNs do sometimes offer an advantage over other investments, their primary value is to give investors exposure to securities that are harder to invest in, such as commodities or emerging markets. For highly liquid indexes such as FANG+, ETNs are just adding more complexity and more risk for a higher cost - especially considering the added risk from being triple leveraged.

We don't see FNGB being worth it and rate it as a sell.

data.title: Jack in the Box: Debt Kills!
data.description:
As a value investor, always hunting for rock-bottom valuations, Jack in the Box (JACK) had been showing up on my stock screeners for quite some time now. The shares have been trading at single-digit P/FCF ratios for months, but it seems like no price is too low, and the share price keeps falling.

In this article, we're going to dive deeper into the reasons why the stock fell off a cliff. I'm also going to explain why I've never pulled the trigger on JACK, even though it's trading at very attractive valuations and has a promising business model.

For those who bought in at the top and went through the pain, we can extract some valuable lessons to prevent it from happening again.

We're also going to cover why I won't buy shares at current valuations, even though JACK is trading at low-single-digit P/FCF ratios, which might seem like a generational bargain.

Jack in the Box Had Potential

Jack in the Box is a fast-food chain founded in 1951 in San Diego, California. The company operates 2,183 Jack in the Box and 591 Del Taco stores in the US. The company tries to get rid of the latter, and the vast majority of those restaurants are franchised.

I'm a European investor, so I have limited experience with the brands itself. The American fast-food chain simply doesn't operate any stores in Europe yet.

However, I remember eating at a Jack in the Box once in 2010, when I visited California as a tourist. On my road trip from Las Vegas to Los Angeles and San Francisco, I came across a few Jack in the Box restaurants and even though I didn't know the brand at that time, I found the store design attractive and gave it a try.

The experience I had was just average. I came in hungry, left a little less hungry, and it didn't break the bank. The food was by no means great, but it was acceptable, just what I'd expect from a fast-food chain.

While the food was just average, the business model that many restaurant chains have is exceptional, and JACK isn't an exception. Out of the 2,774 total stores, 2,511, or 90.52% are franchised.

Owning an operating a restaurant comes with many risks. As primarily a franchisor, JACK transfers most of those risks to the franchisees. It's a wonderful business model for investors.

The franchisees have to endure the struggles that come with operating a restaurant and pay the royalties to JACK, even if they have a bad year and expenses exceeded revenues.

It's an asset-light business model that is highly cash generative and leaves plenty of free cash flow to reinvest or distribute to its shareholders. In theory, JACK should have thrived over the decades, even if the food remained just "average".

Yet here we are, and the share price isn't much higher than it was 25 years ago, so what happened? It comes down to one word: greed.

Jack in the Box is a Business an Idiot Could Run

As a value investor, I'm naturally attracted to the lessons that Warren Buffett has provided over the decades. He's famously known for saying:

I try to invest in businesses that are so wonderful that an idiot can run them. Because sooner or later, one will.

While Jack in the Box and Del Taco probably aren't what Buffett would consider wonderful businesses, I'm sure he loves the business model. And in theory, a company like JACK should do well, even if management did make some mistakes.

But in this case, there were so many bad decisions accumulated over time, that right now the company is struggling under its own debt load. In the worst case, equity investors won't recover from it. In the best case, the painful operational turnaround will take years. What went wrong?

Debt-Funded Buybacks At Elevated Valuations

I am sure many investors will blame the most recent bad management decision, the ill-timed Del Taco acquisition in 2022, for the current share price decline, but that's only half the truth. The decline started to happen long before that, even if the share price didn't reflect it back then.

It's a wonderful (but sometimes painful) lesson to learn from. Many other companies made the same mistake in the easy money environment we had over the last few decades, although JACK took it to the extreme. If we see a sustained period of rising rates, which, I believe, is possible, more and more companies will face the same fate as JACK. So, what did the company do wrong?

The first major mistake management made was excessive buybacks at elevated valuations, funded not by internal cash flows, but by debt.

Don't get me wrong, I like buybacks as long as they're done at reasonable valuations and funded predominantly by free cash flow.

In the case of JACK, the buybacks were neither done at attractive valuations, nor were they funded by cash flows. As long as interest expenses were low, investors cheered, as it led to higher EPS year by year. Now they face a painful reality check.

Shares outstanding went from 42.6 million to 18.83 million in a little over a decade. That means from 2013 to 2024, the company boosted its EPS by 126%, even if the underlying business remained stagnant. And in fact, the business itself didn't grow much.

Cash flows were choppy, but over the decade didn't grow much. Nonetheless, the rising EPS, powered by financial engineering, led investors to believe that JACK had a great future.

Over the same timeframe, the shares appreciated by 139%, reflecting the rising EPS. What investors overlooked was the price the company was paying for its own shares.

JACK did most of the buybacks at a time when the shares were trading at a P/E ratio of 25x+, and sometimes even well above that. We're living in extraordinary times, where those earnings multiples might appear reasonable, but historically the average stock has traded at just 15 to 16 times earnings. I am convinced we'll get back there in the years and decades ahead.

If you're a believer in having a "margin of safety", JACK should have done buybacks whenever shares were trading well below that. While I like the business model, I don't believe the brands and the expected growth rates deserve the premium the management paid for its own shares in all those years. And it now is coming back to haunt them.

Debt levels have been consistently on the rise, but in the good times, investors don't really seem to care. As long as the share price, EPS and dividends go up, and interest rates are low, who cares about ever-rising debt?

Right now, JACK has $1.69 billion of long-term debt and very little cash. Before 2024, the company had roughly an average of $140 million in annual free cash flow.

Banks like to use net debt/EBITDA, but I prefer net debt/FCF, as it's the free cash flow that eventually has the potential to repay the debt. At over 10 years of net debt/FCF, JACK is in deep trouble.

Companies in the fast-food industry with asset-light business models tend to carry more debt than say, a cyclical company, but nonetheless it's excessive. For example, if a company carries more than 4x its FCF in net debt, I start to get a little nervous.

Needless to say, the buybacks left the balance sheet stretched and at a time like now, when buybacks would become attractive, management needed to stop them. It's the classical "buy high, sell low" attitude that leads to disappointing results for many retail investors. Management teams around the world doesn't seem to be immune from making the same mistakes.

Talking about "buying high and selling low" leads us to the next major mistake, the Del Taco acquisition.

Del Taco Acquisition

Back in 2022, JACK decided to acquire Del Taco for $585 million. Just three years later, management tried to get rid of it at a fraction of the price paid. Another classic example of "buying high and selling low".

The restaurant industry is experiencing some challenges and asset prices right now, especially for struggling brands, are at bargain levels. It's not a good time for sellers and I believe given the challenging situation JACK is in, potential buyers will aim for a price well below intrinsic value.

What went wrong here? They massively overpaid for Del Taco, just to get rid of it at any price, because the high debt levels and rising interest rates are forcing them to.

The Del Taco acquisition was indeed ill-timed when it happened back in 2022. It was done at a time when asset prices already recovered and the rising same-store sales after the COVID crash had investors believe they'd continue forever.

But nonetheless, even then, the acquisition was pricey. Del Taco had $957 million in system sales in 2022, so even in that environment, it was far from being a bargain.

As an example, MTY Food Group, which I've covered here, has roughly $4 billion in system sales, and the company trades at just $1.15 billion enterprise value, of course on a debt-free basis. And the brands they hold, on average, are of higher quality than Del Taco. I also believe MTY could be interested in acquiring Del Taco, assuming the price is right.

In summary, we can say the latest acquisition combined with the rising interest rate environment was the final straw that led to the share price decline, which is now approaching almost 90% from the highs.

The share buybacks that happened over the last decade left JACK vulnerable long before that. I believe JACK could be the canary in the coal mine, and we'll see more companies falter from their debt load in the years ahead. Even large companies like Nestlé weren't immune from committing the same mistakes, although on a smaller scale.

Now where does all of this leave us? Could JACK be a great long-term buy and hold at current levels? After all, it has a great business model and is trading at less than 3x P/FCF.

At This Point, JACK Is a Gamble

We didn't cover the operational challenges the company is facing, which is the topic for a following article. But it's safe to say, that at this point, the shares are a gamble, not an investment.

At more than 10x FCF in net debt, the company will take years to lower the debt to more reasonable levels. The cost-of-living crisis, consumers pushing back on higher prices, rising interest rates and more bad management decisions could lead to a disastrous outcome for equity investors pretty quickly.

The planned Del Taco sale leaves many questions, will the company even get $200 million for it, given that the sellers know the company is forced to sell and same-store sales have been in decline?

JACK's interest coverage is dangerously low, and it leaves little room for mistakes at this point. It's safe to say that investors won't receive dividends for the foreseeable future.

That said, at under 3x P/FCF, the shares are exceptionally cheap, and bankruptcy risk appears priced in. The shares have reached the lower range of the 2009 to 2011 levels, which now could act as a support. If I were a holder of JACK, I wouldn't sell my shares at current prices. I'd probably wait for a turnaround, well aware I'm facing the risk of sinking with the ship.

If I were a gambler, I might even pick up some shares. A small positive change could quickly lead to a re-rating, doubling or even tripling the share price from current valuations. I expect this gamble to have a positive expectancy, but the risk of losing it all has to be priced in.

But since I'm a long-term value investor, and risks are too high at this point, I just rate the shares a "hold". I am not a buyer, but I might be a customer and enjoy an "average burger" on my next visit to the United States.

For those of us, who bought in at the top, it might be a good time to review your other holdings. Did any of your companies commit similar mistakes but the share price doesn't reflect it yet? It might be a good time to sell.

Investors interested in JACK, might also want to look at MTY Food Group, I've written several articles about the company. While it's facing challenges and isn't as attractively valued as JACK, in my opinion, it's the better pick on a risk-adjusted basis.

data.title: HubSpot: Recent Selling Amid Strong Billings Is A Good Rebound Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 appears to be stuck in the ~6,000 range, as a confluence of macro factors including rising geopolitical tensions and the Fed's reluctance to lower short-term interest rates are all giving investors balanced reasons to stay invested. But for investors who are inclined to be judicious stock-pickers in this environment, we have a tremendous opportunity to beat the major indices by actively trading positions that are showcasing much more volatility than the calmness of the S&P 500.

HubSpot (NYSE:HUBS) is one of those stocks. The sales and marketing software platform has been among the harder-hit tech stocks this year, with investors sending HubSpot down more than 20% year to date. The stock attempted a brief rebound rally in May after a relatively well-received Q1 earnings print, but since then, the stock has faded noticeably lower.

I downgraded HubSpot to a neutral rating (from a previous buy) just shortly after the stock's brief post-earnings rally to the ~$650s. Since then, HubSpot has slid nearly ~20%. With the stock in correction territory and very little having fundamentally changed in the longer-term bull case for this name, I'm taking advantage of the dip to buy HubSpot again, and am also raising my rating on the stock to a buy.

Valuation Checkup

Since valuation was the core reason behind my decision to lock in gains on HubSpot (and the reason why I'm upgrading the stock back up to a buy), let's start first with a discussion of where HubSpot's multiples are sitting and why it may be a good short-term buy.

At current share prices just over $540, HubSpot trades at a market cap of $28.71 billion. Netting off the $2.22 billion of cash and $368.9 million of convertible debt on HubSpot's latest balance sheet gives us a resulting enterprise value of $26.86 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, HubSpot has guided to $3.036-$3.044 billion in revenue, or 16% y/y growth. For next year FY26, Wall Street analysts have a consensus view of $3.53 billion in revenue for the company, or 16% y/y growth again on top of the midpoint of this year's revenue range.

This puts HubSpot's valuation multiples at:

  • 8.8x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 7.6x EV/FY26 revenue

While it's true that HubSpot isn't exactly a value stock, we note that the stock has routinely commanded low double-digit multiples in the past. In addition, HubSpot is now also trading lower than several key peers, such as Atlassian (TEAM), which is now at a similar growth profile (in fact, Atlassian's growth in its most recent quarter was actually slightly weaker than HubSpot's, at 14% y/y growth). HubSpot is now trading roughly one turn below Atlassian (at the time that I recommended locking in gains and selling HubSpot, it still commanded a premium versus Atlassian).

ServiceNow is another meaningful compare: perennially expensive, this IT service management company grew at 19% y/y in Q1, only a few points faster than HubSpot - and yet, its valuation multiple is still trading ~50% richer.

That being said, not all compares are favorable for HubSpot - Workday (WDAY) is trading well below HubSpot's valuation despite similar growth rates as well though arguably Workday has specific challenges, with AI releases not driving meaningful incremental revenue growth and headcount reductions impacting the HR department users of its HCM software.

I'm not suggesting that HubSpot is capable of delivering tremendous gains and a huge multiple re-rating from here: but in the near term, HubSpot looks oversold with plenty of capacity for a modest rebound. Based on my view of modest upside, my strategy is to sell near-the-money puts and capture generous short-term premiums - though taking a small, short-term buy position in this stock is appropriate as well.

Catalysts to Push HubSpot Higher

HubSpot has been falling in nearly straight sessions over the past few weeks, so it's difficult to recall now that the company delivered quite positive (though not perfect) results in early May.

To me, there are two particularly notable catalysts that, I think, are under-appreciated and may have the potential to push HubSpot higher as soon as next quarter.

The first is that, despite a tough macroeconomy, HubSpot has been able to continue pushing its customer counts higher. In Q1, the company added ~10.4k net-new customers, growing its net customer count by 19% y/y.

Historically, HubSpot is a CRM platform that has appealed more to SMB customers - whereas larger enterprise names gravitate more toward platforms like Salesforce (CRM). SMB customers tend to be more volatile amid a macro downturn: but HubSpot's ability to continue adding new customers at a rapid clip and grow its customer base at nearly ~20% y/y is a strong indication that HubSpot has a strong ability to manage proficiently through a down cycle.

Also, relatively under-appreciated is the fact that HubSpot generated 20% y/y billings growth in Q1 (or 18% on a constant-currency basis). As seasoned software investors are aware, billings is one of the best forward-looking indicators of a recurring-revenue software company's growth trajectory, as it measures deals signed in the quarter that will be recognized as revenue in future quarters.

HubSpot's billings results in Q1 are two points higher than its full-year 16% growth outlook on an FX-neutral basis, and four points higher on an as-reported basis. This is quite a bullish indicator that indicates, at the very least, HubSpot's revenue won't decelerate further from its 16% growth trend in Q1: in fact, if billings trends hold up, revenue growth may even accelerate further into the high teens, giving HubSpot a tailwind to lift its full-year revenue outlook (which would certainly reverse the recent downside in this stock).

Risks and Key Takeaways

In my view, HubSpot is ripe for a near-term bounce back. Of course, investors should take care to consider the risks that the company faces. Average subscription revenue per customer and net retention rates have been slipping slightly, which is an indication that despite HubSpot's strong customer adds, the company may be mixing more into smaller, less reliable customers. We note as well that HubSpot's pro forma operating margins have receded slightly this year (down -100bps to 14.0% in the most recent quarter), driven primarily by the company's decision to boost its 401k match rate (that being said, HubSpot's mid-teens revenue growth plus its mid-teens pro forma operating margin still gives the company a respectable growth/profitability balance).

Again, I'll emphasize that this is a trading position to be monitored carefully in the near term. Investors can stand to benefit from shorter-term plays like capturing premiums from selling puts, but don't get too greedy.

data.title: Old Dominion Freight Line: Valuation More Attractive, But Additional Downside Could Be Near
data.description:
Introduction

Old Dominion Freight Line (NASDAQ:ODFL) for the past year has been plagued by headwinds from higher for longer interest rates. As a result, the LTL truck load carrier has seen rising operating costs and lower freight volumes, both of which have impacted ODFL's financials.

Down double-digits in the past year, their valuation and dividend profile may be particularly attractive for some investors despite my hold rating. However, investors should proceed with caution as the operator could see further downside over the near to medium term.

But long-term, I think ODFL's fundamentals remain solid, and they are a stock worth considering on another 10% - 20% price drop. In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why the company could see additional selling pressure in the coming months.

Previous Thesis

It's been a while since I last covered Old Dominion Freight Line, assigning the stock a hold rating due to their rich valuation. At the time, ODFL had a forward P/E of 37.01x, appearing severely overvalued. But their fundamentals, including a strong balance sheet and payout ratio of just 27% made them attractive on a pullback.

As a result, I suggested investors wait for a potential price correction or market crash before adding. I also assumed ODFL was at high risk to see downside if President Trump implemented tariffs as promised, as these would result in higher costs, likely negatively impacting their financials.

Since, ODFL has pulled back, down over 16% at the time of writing compared to the S&P (SP500), up almost 2.5% over the same period.

Latest Quarter Shows Lingering Headwinds

Old Dominion Freight Line reported their Q1 2025 earnings back in April, and it was apparent the trucking company has been impacted by a weakening economy. Although, EPS of $1.19 managed to beat analysts' estimates, while revenue was in-line at $1.37 billion.

Even though they displayed resilience by rallying after exceeding expectations, their top and bottom lines declined year-over-year. Revenue declined slightly from $1.4 billion, while their bottom line declined double-digits from $1.34.

For comparison, peer J.B. Hunt Transport (JBHT) also experienced a decline, albeit lesser, in their top and bottom lines. Their revenue declined year-over-year from $2.94 billion to $2.92 billion, while earnings per share fell from $1.22 to $1.17. Their operating income fell 8% from $194.4 million to $178.7 million.

Because of tariffs, the market remains on high alert, which has resulted in less than stellar returns in the first 6 months of the year. Many are also wondering how tariffs will impact the Fed's decision regarding lower interest rates.

Below, you can see how Old Dominion's revenue was on an uptrend until 2022, when the Fed decided to hike interest rates. Ever since, revenue has been on a slight decline. But long-term, I expect ODFL to see solid growth once interest rates are much lower, likely over the next 1 to 2 years.

If future tariffs are implemented, or these have a delayed impact, we could see a potential resurgence in inflation sometime in the back half of the year. If so, this will have a negative impact on many companies, including ODFL.

Old Dominion's management attributed their financial decline to a 5% decrease in LTL shipments and 6.3% decrease in LTL tons. But this was partially offset by a 2.2% increase in LTL revenue.

On a positive note, their operating ratio increased 200 basis points year-over-year from 73.4% to 75.4%, a result of their revenue's deleveraging effect on operating expenses.

Going forward, I expect similar performances for ODFL as they remain pressured by higher costs and financially constrained consumers. As a result, freight volumes and demand will likely remain impacted for the near to medium term.

Reduced CAPEX

As a result of a tough macro environment, Old Dominion Freight Line was forced to revise their capital expenditure guidance this year by $125 million to $450 million. The last two years, the company spent a significantly more on investments, amounting to $771 million in 2024 and $757 million in 2023.

Aside from the declining revenue, this is also a tell-tale sign of a tough economic backdrop. Last year, ODFL opened four new service centers and expected to open several more facilities. But until we see a clearer economic backdrop, these will likely be put on hold.

Dividend Profile

Through their tribulations, ODFL's fundamentals have remained solid. Cash from operations, although declining year-over-year, was still solid at $336.5 million. The prior year's quarter saw this at $415.4 million. But as a result of reduced capital expenditures, free cash flow actually came in higher than last year at $248.4 million.

Paying $59.5 million in dividends, this gave ODFL plenty of room for future dividend increases and ensured their dividend will likely remain safe for the foreseeable future.

CAPEX in Q1'24 was $234.7 million, while free cash flow was $180.7 million. For the full-year, free cash flow was $929 million and with CAPEX now reduced, this gives management more retained capital. And this could be used for additional share repurchases for earnings growth, or to pay down long-term debt.

As you can see, Old Dominion's dividend has been well-covered for the past 8 years, giving them plenty of room for future dividend growth, share repurchases, and acquisitions.

Balance Sheet

Old Dominion's balance sheet was solid, with $39.9 billion in long-term debt. However, this may be worrisome for some investors as this currently exceeds their market cap of $33.7 billion.

But long-term debt has improved tremendously over the past 3 years where this stood at roughly $100 billion. Moreover, their ample liquidity after dividend payments gives them additional access to capital to pay down debt over time.

Fairly Valued

After what, I thought, was a justified price correction, ODFL's forward P/E may still be considered high at 30.59x currently. This is down from 37.01x, where they traded at the end of last year. According to FAST Graphs, this suggests ODFL is currently fairly valued but could see potential downside according to their end of year price target of $153.26, which I agree.

As previously mentioned, I think Old Dominion Freight Line will see strong price appreciation over the long term, but due to increased uncertainty that could result in further downside, I now think investors should consider adding below $150.

Risks

Goldman Sachs recently cut its recession probability percentage from 35% to 30%. And while we could avoid one, I think the weakening economy and tariffs could push us into a downturn in the next 12 months.

Moreover, immigration deportations and negative GDP could be a sign of the times. While I'm not predicting a recession, I think chances are higher than 30%, and more around 50% to 60% in the next year.

On the flip side, interest rates will likely remain elevated, at least for the next 6 to 12 months. And while there are two rate cuts expected by the end of this year, I wouldn't be surprised to see one rate cut instead.

Bottom Line

Old Dominion Freight Line, despite their decline year-over-year, has shown resilience as a result of their strong fundamentals. Moreover, their dividend remains as safe due to their reduced spending expected this year.

With a free cash flow payout ratio of less than 30%, this gives the LTL operator more than enough capital to navigate current headwinds and make investments for future growth. As a result of their resilience, the company still remains expensive in my opinion, with a forward P/E above 30x.

While their quality suggests a higher multiple, economic uncertainty could result in further downside, where I think the stock will be much more attractive. For investors looking to add, I suggest doing so on any share price weakness for a margin of safety.

data.title: 3 Big Dividend Income Machines Printing Cash For Retirement
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is one of the best dividend income machines for someone looking to live off cash flow from their investments to fund their retirement. This is because, in addition to offering around a 4% dividend yield, it has a very impressive dividend growth track record of an 11% CAGR over the past decade. This means that not only is its current yield pretty substantial compared to broader market index funds, but it also has a history of growing at a pace that significantly outpaces inflation.

Additionally, its long-term total return performance is pretty solid, indicating that it is more than just a passive income generator but also a wealth compounder over time.

This is in large part thanks to its very low 0.06% expense ratio, as well as its portfolio of over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks.

That being said, SCHD does have some shortcomings, including a lack of much in the way of exposure to infrastructure as well as mega-cap tech stocks, meaning that it may be under-diversified. Additionally, its yield of ~4%, while higher than what you can get from many broadly diversified index funds or even other dividend growth funds like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), is still not terribly high for someone who is in retirement mode and is focused primarily on generating passive income from dividends rather than maximizing capital appreciation.

With these two shortcomings in mind, in this article, I am going to share three big dividend income machines that can significantly enhance a portfolio centered on SCHD from both a yield and diversification standpoint.

Big Dividend Income Machine #1

The first one I am going to discuss is one that provides significant exposure to infrastructure, and is called the Cohen and Steers Infrastructure CEF (UTF). It has a broadly diversified portfolio spread across multiple sectors, including utilities and other infrastructure businesses, including in the industrial and energy spaces. It also has a small amount of fixed income and real estate exposure.

Overall, there is a lot to like as it only trades at a slight premium to its net asset value, meaning that investors do not have to overpay much for the fund. Additionally, it pays out a very attractive 7.2% yield with monthly distributions that have been remarkably consistent over the long-term history of the fund. Moreover, its long-term performance has been very strong, with it outperforming its broader index over the past decade by an annualized rate of about 260 basis points, and it has also outperformed the utilities sector (XLU) over that period:

This demonstrates significant alpha on the part of the management team in allocating capital across the infrastructure space. With this fund, investors can significantly enhance their yield and diversification based on a portfolio centered on SCHD.

Big Dividend Income Machine #2

The next fund I am going to discuss is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). This fund works alongside UTF in providing diversified exposure to infrastructure while also offering an even higher dividend yield of 7.9%. The reason I like AMLP, in addition to its high yield, is that it has strong dividend growth momentum going on right now, as over the past three years it has a 10.06% dividend CAGR, and over the past year, its dividend growth rate has been 8.78%.

Its ability to generate not only a high current yield but inflation-beating dividend growth is likely to continue for the foreseeable future as its top holdings include the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX (MPLX), Plains All-American Pipeline (PAA), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), all of which are likely to grow their dividends at a pace that meets or beats inflation for the foreseeable future. It also has some smaller holdings like Genesis Energy (GEL) and Hess Midstream (HESM) that have also delivered guidance for strong distribution growth in the coming years.

While it is not as well-diversified as UTF is, its higher yield and higher dividend growth potential make AMLP a compelling addition to a retirement income-focused portfolio that wants to increase its exposure to real assets and increase its overall yield while not sacrificing its ability to deliver inflation-beating dividend growth.

Big Dividend Income Machine #3

The third fund I will discuss in this article is the J.P. Morgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). This fund focuses on holding a Nasdaq-100-like underlying portfolio and then implements a notional covered call strategy overlaid on top of it. This is done to generate very attractive monthly distributions, and with an 11.55% trailing 12-month yield, this fund's capacity to deliver very attractive dividends should be evident. With an expense ratio of just 0.35% and the ability to combine yield enhancement with significant diversification enhancement, given that 50% of its holdings are in the mega cap tech space—which the other three funds discussed in this article lack—JEPQ's value to a portfolio like this should be quite evident.

That being said, it is also worth noting that its covered call strategy means that it does not participate much in strong rallies, while still participating fully in the downside of its underlying holdings. This means that it is at risk of long-term NAV erosion. Therefore, I do not think it is prudent to have too much allocated to this fund, and would keep it as a relatively small holding in a portfolio. At the same time, though, the diversification and yield it provides are valuable given that exposure to mega-cap tech is often otherwise lacking in an income-focused portfolio, so JPQ could be a worthwhile holding for that reason alone.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, SCHD can be a very powerful foundational holding in a dividend retirement portfolio. That said, its yield could certainly be higher, and it lacks diversification to real assets like infrastructure and mega-cap tech stocks. To solve these issues, adding funds like UTF, AMLP, and JEPQ can enhance a retirement income portfolio focused on SCHD as its core holding. While we do not invest in many funds and favor picking individual stocks, this sort of balance between yield, dividend growth, and diversification is core to our strategy for formulating our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Negotiating Window For Iran
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Two weeks

President Donald Trump has once again set a two-week timeframe to decide on a complex issue - this time on whether the U.S. should launch an attack on Iran. The deadline is aimed at pressuring Tehran to come to the negotiating table for a nuclear deal, as the escalating Israel-Iran conflict enters its second week.

Diplomacy first: "Based on the fact that there's a substantial chance of negotiations that may or may not take place with Iran in the near future, I will make my decision whether or not to go within the next two weeks," Trump said in a message delivered by White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt. The deadline comes as Israel and Iran continue to exchange attacks by air, targeting military and nuclear sites, as well as civilian infrastructure.

British, French, German and EU foreign ministers will meet with their Iranian counterpart in Geneva on Friday to discuss the situation. "The president supports diplomatic efforts from our allies that could bring Iran closer to taking his deal," a White House official said. While diplomatic efforts are ongoing, Trump weighing a potential strike on Iran has split his support base in the U.S., as many allies want to avoid a repeat of the 2003 invasion of Iraq.

Eye on oil: Iran is reportedly racing to get its oil out into the world, as satellite images show storage tanks at the Kharg Island export terminal are brimming with crude. At the same time, Iran has been driving up its crude exports. This likely means that Iran is directing more crude into the facility to ensure that it can send as much oil as possible to the global market. Meanwhile, energy and shipping industries are closely watching developments at the Strait of Hormuz. The waterway "is the artery through which the world's energy flows, and if that artery is blocked, it'll have a huge impact on global trade," Shell (SHEL) CEO Wael Sawan warned.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

2 BDCs To Buy Before Rates Drop

SCHD: Please Know What You Own

If I Could Only Retire With 3 High Yields Right Now

Nvidia: All-Time High Is In Sight, Upgrade To Strong Buy

Get In Before It's Obvious: The Biggest Bull Market Of The Decade Could Already Be Underway

What else is happening...

SoftBank's Son pitches $1T AI hub to TSMC (TSM), Trump team.

Musk's X eyes in-app stock trading, more consolidation ahead?

Mars mission: SpaceX's (SPACE) Starship explodes during test.

Amazon (AMZN) directs staff to relocate to be near managers.

Trump extends deadline for TikTok (BDNCE) ban for third time.

Bidding war? Home Depot (HD) makes offer for GMS (GMS)

Biggest sports deal: LA Lakers set to be sold at $10B valuation.

Gilead (GILD) wins FDA nod for twice-yearly HIV PrEP shot.

China's rare earth magnet exports slump to five-year low in May.

Swiss central bank's rate hits zero, Bank of England holds steady.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -0.2%. Hong Kong +1.3%. China -0.1%. India +1.3%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.4%. Paris +0.5%. Frankfurt +0.9%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.3%. S&P -0.3%. Nasdaq -0.3%. Crude +0.5% to $73.85. Gold -1% to $3,372.90. Bitcoin +1% to $106,058.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +3 bps to 4.42%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Accenture (ACN) and Kroger (KR).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Mowi: Came For Consumer Products, Stayed For The Secular Moat
data.description:
I am an unsophisticated investor. When I find an interesting product on the store shelves, I go on to research the company producing it and conduct the analysis. That is how I stumbled across Mowi ASA (OTCPK:MHGVY) (OTCPK:MNHVF). Coming back from Japan, I was desperately missing Onigiri (rice ball) snacks in Europe, which were all over Japanese 7-Eleven stores (which I also cover). One day, Mowi Onigiri appeared on European supermarket shelves, albeit with Salmon, a type of fish actually not even common in Japan. Nevertheless, read on to discover what kept me hooked to Mowi from an investor's perspective, far beyond these Consumer Products.

Understanding Mowi

Mowi is the world's largest producer of Atlantic Salmon, and has the complete value chain vertically integrated within the company, including Feed, Breeding, Farming, Harvesting, Processing and Selling including Marketing. However, note that the Feed division is currently under strategic review. Mowi's main market is Europe (66%), followed by America (22%) and Asia (10%).

With its full vertical integration naturally come lots of internal revenues, which also depend on the valuation of goods traded within the company. This, from my perspective, makes it difficult to meaningfully assess segment margins, which I consider a minor issue here. See below, for example, how in the Feed and Farming segments, most revenue is internal, while the Consumer Products segment generates the company's main external revenue. The Markets segment shows an approximate 50:50 split. This also explains why out of roughly 500k tons of produced volume, only 232k tons are reported in the Consumer Products segment. The other half must therefore be sold externally through the Markets segment.

Securing More Of The Rare Production Capacity

Part one of Mowi's value driver equation is ever-increasing production and capacity growth, with a 5% CAGR over the past 6 years to 513k tons on a TTM basis. Norway has held an average of 60% of overall production but contributes an even higher EBIT-share of up to more than 80% in some quarters. The forecasted production CAGR from 2024 to 2029 continues to be 5% to 650k tons. This includes the still ongoing Nova Sea acquisition, which is expected to be closed by H2-2025.

Scarcity-Driven Price

While production volumes depict a long-term CAGR of 5%, revenue grew an average of 7% over the past 6 years due to favorable salmon price developments. But the trend reversed for the time being, with 2% revenue growth against 6% production growth in 2024, as salmon prices decreased. See below the implied realized price in Euro per Kilogram sold by Mowi over time. Over the longer term, however, salmon prices tend to move upwards.

The short-term pressure on prices was due to global supply catching up to record levels recently. But that does not change the management's expectations of prices regaining traction in the long term, starting in 2026 at the latest. This is because Mowi expects the industry to be able to grow supply by only 2-3% annually, lagging global growth in demand. Mowi mentions a historically strong correlation between low supply growth and increasing salmon prices.

The company guides for revenue of €8.5 billion in 2029, implying a 9% top-line CAGR starting from 2024's €5.6 billion. Returning to the anticipated 5% volume CAGR earlier, the remaining 4% annually must come from price increases through branding, product development and the aforementioned scarcity due to limited supply growth. "(…) the industry has reached a production level where biological boundaries are being pushed (…)", Mowi writes in its Salmon Industry Handbook. After all, for optimal development, salmon requires water temperatures of 8 to 14 degrees Celsius and a certain level of current to flow through the farm. These conditions are not found at any given coast and provide a moat for Mowi as the market leader.

Salmon prices also remained more robust than other proteins during the 2023 decline, further cementing its historical position as a rather expensive form of protein. Demand is supported by salmon generally being viewed as healthy, as it is, for example, rich in Omega-3, protein, vitamins, minerals or antioxidants. Mowi also claims that salmon has the best climate footprint among all animal proteins.

Production Costs

Mowi's main P&L line item is Cost of Materials with 50% of revenue, mainly consisting of Feed; followed by personnel expenses and other operating expenses, all at comparably low levels, well below 20%. For the first quarter, Mowi reported blended farming costs of €5.89 per kilogram, down from €6.05 a year ago. This represents a welcome downward trend after inflationary spikes starting in the 2021 post-Covid period. Higher volumes should lead to continued production cost decreases in Q2 and H2, according to Mowi.

The company also reported cost improvement potential of €300-400 million. Related to 2029's expected revenue of €8.5 billion, this would translate to a margin improvement of 4 percentage points. See below, that major margin improvements are already factored into analysts' forecasts.

Consumer Products

Mowi's Consumer Products likely capture the industry's Zeitgeist, and they were the starting point of my analysis of Mowi. However, they are not what keep me particularly interested in the company, as operating margins in the segment are in razor-thin, low single digit margin territory, albeit improving to 3.7% in Q1-2025, up from 2.7% in Q1-2024. Volume was up 3% while revenue was essentially flat due to an adverse product mix.

Risk Summary

Net Debt has been raised as a reason for concern among some analysts. However, the company's Net Debt to EBITDA ratio of 2.9 on a TTM basis according to Seeking Alpha appears manageable, as does an interest coverage ratio of 4.8 (EBIT / Net Interest Expenses) for the moment. The company's cash balance is typically low due to high net working capital requirements and lengthy farming cycles, paired with growth efforts.

Externally, salmon health and conditions such as seawater temperatures pose constant risks to the business.

Valuation Is Not An Obstacle

Mowi's long-term earnings and revenue CAGRs resemble those of its Norwegian peers, as largely do the expected ones. Refer to the table below for details. On a 2027 forward earnings basis, Mowi trades at a 10x multiple today, within the peers' forward P/E range of 7 to 11. This indicates a fair valuation, making it an adequate entry point.

Against the background of the expected scarcity-driven price dynamics in favor of salmon producers, I am considering initiating a position in this global market leader. The reason for the still cautious Hold rating is that its commodity-like business model is not what I have high conviction about in a classic buy-and-hold portfolio. Also, regulatory and climate related risks including optimal farming conditions keep me from rating an unrestricted Buy. Still, my inclination leans positive.
